Question: What is the name of the outermost layer of the skin?
Subject: D - Histology & Integumentary System
SLO Description: Describe the functions and structure of the epidermis.
Explanation: The epidermis is the superficial outermost layer of the skin composed of keratinized stratified squamous epithelium.

Question: Which of the following is NOT correct concerning the skin?
Option B: Epidermis composed of stratified squamous epithelium
Option C: Subcutaneous layer is between dermis and epidermis
Option D: Dermis contains smooth muscle and nerve tissue
Correct Answer: Subcutaneous layer is between dermis and epidermis
Subject: D - Histology & Integumentary System
SLO Description: Describe the functions and structural relationships of skin layers.
Explanation: The subcutaneous layer (hypodermis) lies deep to the dermis anchoring the skin to underlying tissues; it is not located between the dermis and epidermis.

Question: Hair and fingernails are derived from _______ cells
Subject: D - Histology & Integumentary System
SLO Description: Explain the accessory structures derived from the epidermis.
Explanation: Hair follicles and nails are accessory integumentary structures formed from specialized downward invaginations of epidermal epithelial cells.

Question: Which of the following is a normal response to excessive loss of body heat in a cold environment?
Option D: Dermal blood vessels release heat to exterior
Subject: D - Histology & Integumentary System
SLO Description: Describe thermoregulatory mechanisms of the integumentary system.
Explanation: When body temperature drops involuntary skeletal muscle contractions (shivering) produce metabolic heat to preserve thermal homeostasis.

Question: When the smooth muscle in the walls of dermal blood vessels contract
Subject: D - Histology & Integumentary System
SLO Description: Explain how skin blood flow maintains body temperature homeostasis.
Explanation: Vasoconstriction of dermal blood vessels reduces blood flow to the skin surface conserving core body heat during cold exposure.

Question: A burn that involves only the epidermis is called a
Subject: D - Histology & Integumentary System
SLO Description: Distinguish between degrees of burns and their physiological effects.
Explanation: A first-degree (superficial partial-thickness) burn injures only the superficial epidermis causing local redness and mild pain without blistering.

Question: In treating a burned patient the "rule of nines" is used to estimate the
Option D: type of scar that will form
Subject: D - Histology & Integumentary System
SLO Description: Explain clinical assessment of burn injuries using the rule of nines.
Explanation: The rule of nines divides body regions into anatomical zones of 9% to quickly estimate the percentage of total body surface area affected by burns.

Question: Which layer of the epidermis is present only in thick skin such as the palms of the hands and soles of the feet?
Subject: D - Histology & Integumentary System
SLO Description: Identify the five layers of the epidermis and their locations.
Explanation: Stratum lucidum is a clear translucent extra layer of dead keratinocytes found only in thick hairless skin of palms and soles.

Question: Melanin the primary pigment responsible for skin color is produced by melanocytes located in which epidermal layer?
Subject: D - Histology & Integumentary System
SLO Description: Explain the origin location and function of melanocytes.
Explanation: Melanocytes reside in the deep stratum basale synthesizing melanin to shield keratinocyte nuclear DNA from ultraviolet radiation damage.

Question: Fingerprints and epidermal ridges are produced by projections of which upper layer of the dermis?
Subject: D - Histology & Integumentary System
SLO Description: Describe the structural organization of the papillary and reticular dermal layers.
Explanation: The superficial papillary layer of the dermis contains dermal papillae that project into the epidermis creating friction ridges that form fingerprints.

Question: Sebaceous glands are exocrine glands associated with hair follicles that secrete an oily substance called
Subject: D - Histology & Integumentary System
SLO Description: Describe cutaneous exocrine glands and their secretions.
Explanation: Sebaceous glands release sebum (an oily lipid mixture) via holocrine secretion into hair follicles to keep skin pliable and waterproof.

Question: Which sweat glands are widely distributed throughout the body and function primarily in evaporative cooling and thermoregulation?
Subject: D - Histology & Integumentary System
SLO Description: Distinguish between eccrine and apocrine sweat glands.
Explanation: Eccrine (merocrine) sweat glands secrete watery sweat onto the skin surface to cool the body when internal temperature rises.

Question: Goosebumps are caused by the contraction of which smooth muscle bundles attached to hair follicles?
Subject: D - Histology & Integumentary System
SLO Description: Identify accessory structures associated with hair follicles.
Explanation: Arrector pili smooth muscles contract under sympathetic stimulation during cold or emotional responses causing hair to stand upright.

Question: Epidermal cells exposed to ultraviolet (UV) radiation convert a cholesterol precursor molecule into which essential nutrient precursor?
Subject: D - Histology & Integumentary System
SLO Description: Explain metabolic and synthetic functions of skin including vitamin synthesis.
Explanation: UV light stimulates conversion of 7-dehydrocholesterol in skin cells to Vitamin D precursor (cholecalciferol) essential for intestinal calcium absorption.

Question: Meissner's (tactile) corpuscles in the dermal papillae are specialized sensory receptors for detecting
Subject: D - Histology & Integumentary System
SLO Description: Identify sensory receptors in skin and their modalities.
Explanation: Tactile (Meissner's) corpuscles are encapsulated nerve endings in the dermal papillae designed for detecting light touch and fine texture.
